title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who talk about their connection to nature and animals. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Wild at Heart: Four People on Animals and Nature

texts.A:
### Nina, 30, Veterinary Nurse
[43]I have worked with animals since I was sixteen and I cannot imagine any other profession.[/43] People sometimes assume it's a gentle, easy job, but it can be emotionally exhausting – losing a patient is never routine, even after years. [47]The hardest aspect is often dealing with the owners rather than the animals.[/47] People's grief when they lose a pet is completely genuine and supporting them is as important as treating the animal. [51]Outside work, I have two rescue dogs and a garden full of bird feeders – bringing nature home makes everything more peaceful.[/51]
texts.B:
### Robert, 67, Retired Farmer
[44]I farmed the same land for over forty years and I know every field, every hedgerow, every season's particular character.[/44] [48]I've watched the wildlife on my land change dramatically over my lifetime – there are birds I saw as a child that are now almost impossible to spot.[/48] That saddens me deeply. Since retiring I've turned a large part of my land into a wildlife meadow and the change has been remarkable – the insects came back first, then the birds followed. [52]I don't think farming and nature have to be in opposition, but it requires a long-term view that doesn't always fit with the economics of modern agriculture.[/52]
texts.C:
### Sasha, 23, Environmental Science Graduate
[45]I grew up in a city and had almost no contact with nature as a child – the first time I saw a deer in the wild, I was nineteen and on a university field trip.[/45] It was a profound experience. [49]Since then I've become passionate about urban rewilding – the idea that nature can come back into cities if we give it space.[/49] I volunteer with a project planting native wildflowers on unused land in my city. [53]I believe access to nature is a social justice issue – wealthier areas have parks and green spaces while poorer communities often have nothing.[/53]
texts.D:
### Ellie, 11, Primary School Pupil
[46]My favourite thing is going rock pooling at the beach near my grandmother's house.[/46] You have to be really patient and quiet, and then suddenly you see crabs and sea anemones and tiny fish. It's like discovering a hidden world every time. [50]My teacher says I know more about marine life than most adults, which makes me very proud.[/50] [54]I want to be a marine biologist when I grow up because I think the ocean is the most mysterious place on Earth and there's so much we still don't know about it.[/54] My parents say I'm too young to decide, but I don't think I'll ever change my mind.

questions.question: has always worked with animals and cannot picture doing anything else?
questions.explanation: Nina has worked with animals since she was sixteen and cannot imagine any other profession.

questions.question: has an intimate, long-standing knowledge of a particular piece of land?
questions.explanation: Robert farmed the same land for over forty years and knows every field, hedgerow and seasonal character.

questions.question: had very little experience of nature during their childhood?
questions.explanation: Sasha grew up in a city with almost no contact with nature and first saw a deer in the wild at nineteen.

questions.question: enjoys an outdoor activity that requires stillness and patience to observe wildlife?
questions.explanation: Ellie loves rock pooling, which requires being patient and quiet to spot crabs, sea anemones and tiny fish.

questions.question: finds a particular aspect of their job unexpectedly challenging that involves people rather than animals?
questions.explanation: Nina says the hardest aspect of her job is often dealing with the owners rather than the animals.

questions.question: has personally witnessed a decline in wildlife over a period of time?
questions.explanation: Robert has watched wildlife on his land change dramatically and says there are birds he saw as a child that are now almost impossible to spot.

questions.question: is interested in helping nature return to urban environments?
questions.explanation: Sasha is passionate about urban rewilding and volunteers with a project planting native wildflowers on unused city land.

questions.question: has been praised for having exceptional knowledge of wildlife for their age?
questions.explanation: Ellie's teacher says she knows more about marine life than most adults.

questions.question: has created a welcoming environment for wildlife at their own home?
questions.explanation: Nina has two rescue dogs and a garden full of bird feeders, bringing nature home.

questions.question: suggests that two things which are often seen as opposed can in fact coexist?
questions.explanation: Robert says farming and nature don't have to be in opposition but require a long-term view.
